meta.title: X118 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine — M282, M260, M139, OM608, OM654. Real UK costs, the M282 oil consumption watchpoint, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X118 engine typically costs £1,500–£9,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume M282 four‑cylinder petrol sits at £1,800–£3,200, while the AMG M139 V8‑rivalling four commands £5,500–£9,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel (200 d/220 d) is the most robust — it has proven itself across the Mercedes range with fewer major issues than the M282 petrol. Among petrols, the M260 2.0‑litre turbo (CLA 250) is the most dependable, with timing chain stretch its only real watchpoint.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil consumption and timing chain stretch on the M282 1.3‑litre petrol, AdBlue system failures on the OM654 diesel, timing chain stretch on the M260 2.0‑litre, and sensor‑derived misfire issues on the high‑strung M139 AMG engine. The OM608 diesel is generally basic but can suffer DPF clogging.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine. The M260 and OM654 are solid choices. The M282 (Renault‑developed 1.3‑litre) has the most owner‑reported issues, particularly oil consumption. The AMG M139 is reliable for a high‑performance engine but demands meticulous maintenance. Choose wisely based on your budget and priorities.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a pre‑facelift M282 without documented oil consumption checks is the one we consistently advise buyers to inspect carefully. A neglected M139 AMG without full service history is the second, as sensor repairs can be expensive.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Excessive oil consumption (M282), cold‑start rattle (M260/M282 timing chain), AdBlue countdown warnings (OM654), or misfires under load (M139) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £18,000 X118, a £4,000 OM654 recon is 22% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 50% — still under the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold, but borderline. Value the car and its history first.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing, though AdBlue maintenance is recurring. The M260 petrol can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles with regular oil changes. The M282 has a more variable record — some exceed 100,000 miles without issues, others need attention earlier.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X118 Shooting Brake and the C118 Saloon?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are second‑generation CLA‑Class, sharing the MFA2 platform and all engines. The X118 is the 5‑door estate/Shooting Brake (2019–2025, this page), while the C118 is the 4‑door saloon (2019–2025). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (CLA 180, 200, 250, AMG 35, AMG 45, or diesel) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X118 Shooting Brake — from the 1.3‑litre M282 to the 2.0‑litre M139 in the AMG 45 S. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the powertrain choices that define Mercedes' compact estate. Backed by real UK X118 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class X118 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X118 Shooting Brake specifically — from the entry‑level M282 to the fire‑breathing M139 in the AMG 45 S.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM654: 95% confidence — inherited from the EM_Proprietary_Data Known Failure Patterns table. M282, M260, M139 and OM608: Expert‑knowledge tier, derived from specialist consensus. All ratings are PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Pages for each family. M282 (Renault‑developed 1.3‑litre): known for occasional oil consumption and timing chain issues; generally robust for a small turbo petrol. OM654: generally reliable, with AdBlue system issues the main concern. M139: the most powerful four‑cylinder production engine; known for high‑strung nature and tight engine bay packaging.

overview.intro: The X118 is the Shooting Brake (estate) bodystyle of the second‑generation Mercedes‑Benz CLA‑Class, launched in 2019 as the successor to the X117. It shares the MFA2 platform with the A‑Class (W177) and GLA (H247), offering a more practical estate body while retaining the CLA's sleek, coupé‑like roofline. A significant 2023 facelift brought mild‑hybrid technology to most petrol engines, improved infotainment, and updated styling. Every X118 is powered by a transversely‑mounted four‑cylinder engine, driving the front wheels or 4MATIC all‑wheel drive.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz MFA2 (Modular Front Architecture) • Predecessor: X117 • Successor: X178 (2025‑on) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C118 Saloon.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The second‑generation CLA‑Class (C118/X118 combined) generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], with the OM654 diesel and M260 petrol commanding the strongest specialist interest.

items.quote: The modern 2.0‑litre diesel that replaced the ageing OM651. Excellent fuel economy, strong torque (400Nm), and none of the injector seal issues that plagued its predecessor. AdBlue system niggles are its only real demand.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want genuine diesel efficiency without the OM608's compromises.

items.quote: Mercedes' 2.0‑litre turbo four — smooth, responsive, and fundamentally robust. Delivers 224hp with genuine everyday usability, and shares its architecture with the AMG 35 for proven durability.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want everyday performance without the AMG premium.

items.quote: The Renault‑developed 1.3‑litre turbo is the most contentious engine in the range — occasional oil consumption and timing chain concerns have been reported, and it lacks the refinement of the 2.0‑litre M260.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a tight budget who prioritise running costs over driving experience.

items.quote: The most powerful four‑cylinder production engine in the world. High‑strung design and tight engine bay packaging make even sensor replacement labour‑intensive — and a full recon is a five‑figure bill.
items.whoItsFor: AMG buyers with deep pockets and a trusted specialist.

items.quote: The entry‑point to the proven OM654 diesel family. 150hp and 400Nm deliver strong real‑world performance, and the engine shares its robust design with the 220 d — just with lower running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy without the higher purchase price of the 220 d.

items.quote: The 35 sits in a sweet spot between the 250 and the full‑fat 45 — 306hp, 4MATIC, and the same M260 architecture that underpins the non‑AMG cars. Lower running costs than the 45, and more accessible maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance without the 45's maintenance premium.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Entry‑Level — budget for potential oil use
Entry Diesel — cheap to run, basic performance
Best Diesel — strong torque, same costs

economicsRule.title: The X118 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £15,000–£25,000 X118 sits at 12–33% of the car's value — well inside the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Against a £10,000 high‑mileage example, the same recon reaches 30–50% — still viable, but a significant proportion of the car's residual value. Value the car and its service history first.

problems.title: M282 1.3‑litre Turbo Oil Consumption
problems.rootCause: The Renault‑developed M282 engine can suffer from piston ring wear leading to increased oil consumption — some owners report needing a litre of oil between services. Timing chain stretch is also reported on higher‑mileage examples.
problems.recommendation: Monitor oil levels regularly — a £600–£1,200 preventative inspection is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,000 replacement that follows oil starvation.

cta.label: Compare M282 engine prices for your X118 →

problems.title: OM654 AdBlue System Failure
problems.affectedModels: X118 CLA 200 d, CLA 220 d (all OM654.920).
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue system's NOx sensors, heater elements, and dosing valves can fail — leading to countdown warnings that prevent the car from restarting.
problems.recommendation: A £500–£1,500 AdBlue system service is non‑negotiable on the OM654 — the countdown warnings give you limited mileage before the car won't restart.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your X118 →

problems.title: M260 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The 2.0‑litre turbo's timing chain can stretch over time, particularly on engines with extended service intervals — a rattle on cold start is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £3,000–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M260 engine prices for your X118 →

problems.title: M139 High‑Strung Nature / Sensor Misfires
problems.affectedModels: X118 AMG CLA 45, CLA 45 S (all M139.980).
problems.rootCause: The most powerful four‑cylinder production engine in the world is a high‑strung design — tight engine bay packaging makes even sensor replacement labour‑intensive, and misfire issues can be expensive to diagnose.
problems.recommendation: On a £25,000–£40,000 AMG CLA 45 S, a £1,500–£3,000 sensor diagnosis is cheap insurance — it protects the £7,000–£10,000 engine that sits at the heart of the car.

cta.label: Compare M139 engine prices for your AMG CLA 45 →
